summary: Joseph Anthony Grande was my great-great-grandfather, born in Italy on July 8th, 1898. He was the oldest of eight siblings and the only one born in Italy before the family immigrated to New Jersey in the early 1900s. Joseph worked at the Aluminum Company of America and was involved in trying to patent the aluminum can. He married Theresa Cometot in the 1920s, had six children, and lived a life shaped by the Americanization Movement. He passed away in New Jersey in 1962, leaving behind a legacy as a father, businessman, and Catholic leader.

Italy is where my great-great-grandfather Joseph Anthony Grande was born in 1898. It was also the homeland of many Italian immigrants, including Joseph's parents who left due to poverty and poor living conditions. Italy's culture and heritage remained important to the family even after they settled in New Jersey. My great uncles maintained strong ties to their Italian roots, especially through their Catholic faith and visits to places like the Vatican in Rome. Italy represents the beginning of our family's journey before the American dream.

New Jersey was the place where my great-great-grandfather Joseph Anthony Grande and his family settled after immigrating from Italy in the early 1900s. It was where Joseph grew up, worked at the Aluminum Company of America, and raised his six children with Theresa Cometot. The family lived comfortably there by 1935, owning a nice house and a luxury Packard car. New Jersey was also the site of the scrap metal business run by Joseph's sons after World War II, which was tied to the Italian Mafia. Joseph died in New Jersey in 1962, marking the end of his chapter in our family history.

Italian Americans like my family came to the United States in huge numbers during the early 20th century, seeking better lives away from poverty in Italy. My great-great-grandfather Joseph Grande's family was part of this wave, settling in New Jersey and embracing the American dream. Italian Americans faced challenges but also contributed greatly to American society, including through work at companies like Ford and Alcoa. Our family story reflects the broader Italian American experience of assimilation, cultural retention, and economic striving. Today, Italian Americans are the fifth largest ethnic group in the U.S., a testament to their enduring presence.

The Americanization Movement was a big influence on my great-great-grandfather Joseph Grande's life as he grew up in New Jersey during the early 1900s. It aimed to help immigrants like the Grande family assimilate into American culture by educating them about customs and policies. This movement shaped Joseph's career, lifestyle, and family values as he worked to achieve the American dream. It was part of the broader context of mass Italian immigration and the challenges newcomers faced. The movement helped Joseph and his family integrate while still holding onto their Italian heritage.

Theresa Cometot was my great-great-grandmother, whom Joseph Grande met and married in the 1920s. She was born in Pennsylvania to parents from Calabria, Italy, and was part of the Italian immigrant community like Joseph. Theresa worked at the Ford Motor Company and together they had five sons and one daughter. By 1935, they had achieved a comfortable life in New Jersey, embodying the American dream their parents hoped for. Theresa's steady work and family life were central to our family's story.

Pennsylvania is where my great-great-grandmother Theresa Cometot was born before she moved to New Jersey. Her parents were originally from Calabria, Italy, and like many Italian immigrants, they settled in the U.S. seeking better opportunities. Theresa later worked at the Ford Motor Company, which was a significant part of her life and the family's economic stability. Pennsylvania represents the starting point of Theresa's American journey before joining the Grande family in New Jersey.

The Ford Motor Company was where my great-great-grandmother Theresa Cometot worked, especially around the 1940s when she was earning about $925 a year. It was an important source of income for our family during and after World War II. Ford represented industrial opportunity for many Italian Americans like Theresa, who balanced work with raising a large family. The company was part of the broader American industrial landscape that shaped our family's experience. Theresa's employment there helped support the household while Joseph worked at Alcoa.

Alcoa, or the Aluminum Company of America, was where my great-great-grandfather Joseph Grande worked during the early to mid-20th century. Joseph was part of an elite group trying to patent the aluminum can, though the patent ultimately failed. This setback caused the family to lose much of their wealth, but Alcoa remained central to their livelihood. After World War II, Joseph's sons started a scrap metal business connected to aluminum sourced from Alcoa shipments in New Jersey. Alcoa was a key part of our family's industrial and economic history.

The Packard Motor Car Company is remembered in my family because my great-great-grandfather Joseph Grande drove a blue Packard around New Jersey in the 1930s. Owning such a luxury car symbolized the success Joseph and Theresa had achieved by 1935, fulfilling their parents' hopes for the American dream. The Packard was more than just a car; it was a status symbol that reflected the family's upward mobility during that era. It remains a vivid image of the Grande family's prosperity before financial difficulties arose.

World War II was a pivotal time for my family as all five of Joseph Grande's sons served in the military during the conflict. The war years were challenging but also marked a period of service and sacrifice for our family. After the war ended around 1945, Joseph's sons returned home and started a scrap metal business that was connected to aluminum and, unfortunately, tied to the Italian Mafia. The war shaped their lives and the family's post-war economic activities. It was a defining chapter in our family's history.

The Italian Mafia became involved with my great uncles Robert and Paul Grande after World War II when they started a scrap metal business dealing in aluminum. This business involved robbing ships of aluminum from the Aluminum Company of America in New Jersey and reselling it, which led to frequent government raids. Despite the legal troubles, the family had people who warned them in advance so they could hide the aluminum. The Mafia connection was a complicated and risky part of our family's post-war history, eventually ending with my great uncles. It reflects the darker side of the immigrant experience and economic survival.

Vatican City holds a special place in our family's religious history because my great uncle Paul Grande frequently visited there. He would sing for the pope during his visits, which was a great honor and a reflection of the family's deep Catholic faith. These trips connected our family to the heart of Catholicism and reinforced our spiritual traditions. When he returned to New Jersey, he would sing sacred songs like 'Ave Maria' for my grandma and dad. Vatican City symbolizes the religious devotion that has been a cornerstone of the Grande family.

Rome is significant in our family story because it is the city where the Vatican is located, which my great uncle Paul Grande visited often. During these visits, he sang for the pope, an experience that deeply connected him to our Catholic heritage. Rome represents a spiritual and cultural link between our Italian roots and our life in America. The songs he brought back, like 'Ave Maria,' became cherished family traditions in New Jersey. Rome is a symbol of faith and identity for the Grande family.

'Ave Maria' is a song that my great uncle Paul Grande used to sing for our family after returning from his visits to the Vatican in Rome. It became a meaningful reminder of our Catholic faith and the family's Italian heritage. Hearing 'Ave Maria' at home in New Jersey connected us to the sacred traditions and the spiritual journey of our ancestors. The song symbolizes the religious devotion that has been passed down through generations in the Grande family. It remains a cherished part of our family's history and identity.
